key_points: The lithosphere is broken into a number of plates known as Lithospheric plates.
These plates move very slowly—just a few millimeters each year—because of the movement of the molten magma inside the earth.
The forces that act in the interior of the earth are called Endogenic forces.
The forces that work on the surface of the earth are called Exogenic forces.
Sudden movements like earthquakes and volcanoes cause mass destruction over the surface of the earth.
examples: Endogenic Forces: Earthquakes, Volcanoes, and Landslides.
Exogenic Forces: River, Wind, Sea waves, and Glaciers (Erosional and Depositional).
Volcano: An opening (vent) in the earth's crust through which molten material erupts suddenly.
Earthquake: Vibration of the earth's surface when lithospheric plates move.
formulas: Earth Movements = Endogenic Forces (Internal) + Exogenic Forces (External)
Lithospheric Plate Movement = Effect of Molten Magma (Circular motion)
exam_tips: Remember that the point where the vibration starts inside the crust is called the Focus.
The place on the surface above the focus is called the Epicenter.
Understand that 'Endo' means inside and 'Exo' means outside.
Note that the Earth's plates move because the molten magma inside moves in a circular manner.
